talk_title: STING agonists amplify BH3 mimetic activity via p53- and cGAS-STING-dependent apoptosis loops

biomarkers.name: p53 status
biomarkers.finding: p53 loss correlates with reduced amplification loop and resistance to BH3 mimetics

biomarkers.name: STING expression
biomarkers.finding: High STING levels predict sensitivity to STING agonist killing in AML samples

models.name: Rag knockout mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: immune deficient, engrafted with Cas9-expressing lymphoma

clinical_data.value: 50% of mice survived with dual treatment
clinical_data.comparator: STING agonist alone or MCL-1 inhibitor alone

clinical_data.population: Cas9 transgenic mice with p53 knockout lymphoma
human AML PDX treated with venetoclax + STING agonist

MOA_summary: STING agonists activate cGAS-STING signaling leading to IRF3-dependent transcription of BH3-only proteins (BIM, NOXA, PUMA) and p53 activation, creating a feed‑forward loop that amplifies apoptosis; combined with BH3 mimetics (venetoclax or MCL‑1 inhibitors) this overcomes resistance by releasing BIM from anti‑apoptotic proteins, resulting in enhanced tumor cell killing in vitro and in mouse models.
key_findings: STING agonists induce p53‑independent IRF3 activation and upregulation of BIM, NOXA, and PUMA in lymphoma and leukemia cells.
Loss of Bax/Bak abolishes STING‑agonist‑mediated apoptosis, confirming dependence on the intrinsic pathway.
Combination of STING agonist with BH3 mimetics (MCL‑1 inhibitor or venetoclax) yields synergistic killing in vitro and improves survival in immune‑deficient and immune‑competent mouse models.
High STING expression in AML patient samples predicts sensitivity to STING agonist monotherapy and to the combination with venetoclax.
In vivo, dual treatment leads to ~50% survival benefit in Rag‑KO mice and extends median survival three‑fold in Cas9‑transgenic mice.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: p53 loss reduces the amplification loop, conferring resistance to BH3 mimetics.
BCL2 point mutations can abrogate venetoclax binding while preserving BH3‑only protein interaction.

combination_strategies.rationale: STING agonist upregulates BH3‑only proteins, increasing dependence on anti‑apoptotic proteins that are blocked by BH3 mimetics.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor cell killing and improved mouse survival

open_questions: How can STING expression be increased in cancer cells with low baseline STING levels?
What are the precise molecular mechanisms linking mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization to p53 activation?

talk_title: Mitochondrial priming, BCL2 family dependencies and resistance mechanisms in multiple myeloma

biomarkers.name: BIM (BCL2L11) expression
biomarkers.finding: Higher BIM levels correlate with increased mitochondrial priming and venetoclax sensitivity

biomarkers.name: IL6 levels
biomarkers.finding: Stromal IL6 induces resistance to BCL2 inhibition by shifting BIM binding to MCL1

biomarkers.name: BIM phosphorylation (Ser94)
biomarkers.finding: Phosphorylation at Ser94 reduces sensitivity to MCL1 inhibitor AZD5991

biomarkers.name: t(11;14) translocation
biomarkers.finding: Patients with t(11;14) show higher ex‑vivo venetoclax sensitivity (≈40% response vs 6% without)

models.name: KMS18
models.genotype_or_key_features: BCL2‑bound BIM, shifts to MCL1 upon venetoclax

models.name: venetoclax trial cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: n≈70, includes t(11;14) and non‑t(11;14) patients

clinical_data.value: 40% in t(11;14) patients vs 6% in non‑t(11;14)
clinical_data.population: multiple myeloma patients treated with venetoclax

clinical_data.value: significantly longer in venetoclax‑sensitive ex‑vivo group

MOA_summary: Myeloma cells exhibit high mitochondrial priming driven by BIM up‑regulation; venetoclax releases BIM from BCL2 but BIM can be re‑sequestered by MCL1, especially after IL6‑mediated signaling or BIM phosphorylation, leading to resistance. Combination with dexamethasone or IL6 blockade restores priming and sensitizes cells, while t(11;14) myeloma shows intrinsic BCL2 dependence and higher venetoclax response.
key_findings: BIM is up‑regulated in myeloma and drives mitochondrial priming, making cells vulnerable to BH3 mimetics.
Venetoclax treatment releases BIM from BCL2, which then binds MCL1, causing resistance in BCL2‑dependent lines.
Dexamethasone increases total BIM and restores venetoclax sensitivity across resistant myeloma samples.
Stromal IL6 shifts BIM binding to MCL1, conferring resistance; IL6 neutralization or Tocilizumab pretreatment re‑sensitizes cells to venetoclax.
BIM phosphorylation at Ser94 (and Ser87) reduces sensitivity to MCL1 inhibition, indicating a post‑translational resistance mechanism.
t(11;14) myeloma shows higher venetoclax sensitivity linked to a B‑cell‑like differentiation state rather than BCL2 expression levels.
Acquired BCL2 point mutations (e.g., G101V) after therapy diminish venetoclax binding and drive clinical resistance.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: IL6‑mediated signaling induces rapid, post‑translational adaptation increasing MCL1 and shifting BIM binding, leading to venetoclax resistance.
BIM phosphorylation (Ser94) creates resistance to MCL1 inhibitors.
Acquired BCL2 mutations (G101V) impair venetoclax efficacy while preserving BIM binding.

combination_strategies.rationale: Increases BIM transcription, enhancing priming and venetoclax efficacy
combination_strategies.phenotype: Conversion of venetoclax‑resistant cells to sensitive; higher ORR in early phase trial

combination_strategies.rationale: Blocks stromal IL6 signaling that shifts BIM to MCL1
combination_strategies.phenotype: Restores venetoclax sensitivity in vitro and in patient‑derived samples

combination_strategies.rationale: Dual inhibition of BCL2 and MCL1 overcomes BIM redistribution
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic killing in cell lines; resistance observed with BIM phosphorylation

open_questions: What kinases mediate BIM Ser94 phosphorylation and can they be targeted to prevent resistance?
How does Ras activation confer venetoclax resistance independent of MCL1 up‑regulation?
